source_metadata.title_raw: Terry E. Dixon - A-18-205 - November 1, 2018 - Laguna Niguel

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Terry E. Dixon 
City Attorney 
City of Laguna Niguel 
30111 Crown Valley Parkway 
Laguna Niguel, CA 92677 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-205 
Dear Mr. Dixon: 
November 1, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Laguna Niguel Mayor Pro Tern 
Mark Jennings, regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note 
that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and 
any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the 
facts underlying these decisions should change, contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are performance tickets offered to the Mayor Pro Tern in exchange for his participation as a 
member of the Board of Directors for Pacific Symphony, a non-profit organization, considered gifts 
for purposes of the Act? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. As a Pacific Symphony Board member, the tickets provided to the Mayor Pro Tern for 
performances are considered gifts, subject to the gift limit, and must be reported on his statement of 
economic interests if the Mayor Pro Tern uses the tickets or transfers the tickets to his guests. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY THE REQUESTOR 
The City of Laguna Niguel is a member of the Association of California Cities Orange 
County ("ACCOC"). Mayor Pro Tern Mark Jennings has been appointed by the ACCOC to serve 
on the Board of Directors for the Pacific Symphony. The mission of the Pacific Symphony, from its 
website, is to inspire, engage, and serve Orange County and the region through exceptional musical 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-205 
Page No. 2 
performances, and education and community programing. In an e-mail exchange from October 9, 
2018, you confirmed that Pacific Symphony is a 50l(c)(3) nonprofit organization. 
Approximately, 85 people serve on the Pacific Symphony Board of Directors. The position 
is primarily utilized for public relations and fundraising purposes. As a member of the Board of 
Directors, Pacific Symphony proposes that it would give the Mayor Pro Tern access to four tickets 
per performance to attend various Pacific Symphony performances. The tickets are made available 
to him for the purpose and intent that he use the tickets to take two people, preferably elected 
officials or city managers from around Orange County, to Pacific Symphony performances to 
encourage 'those people to attend, contribute to, and support the Pacific Symphony and its 
community-based endeavors. 
The sole purpose of the Mayor Pro Tern's appointment to the Pacific Symphony Board was 
to enhance the Pacific Symphony's community outreach, which will consume the Mayor Pro Tern's 
time and efforts to coordinate attendance and educate his guests on the programs and benefits 
Pacific Symphony does in the community. 
The face value of the tickets varies by the performance and location. It is anticipated that the 
total value of any four of the tickets to a single performance may not exceed $470, the current gift 
limit. However, it is also anticipated that the Mayor Pro Tern would receive four tickets for multiple 
performances all(jl, if the value of the tickets for the first performance does not exceed $4 70, the 
cumulative value of multiple performances would exceed the amount. 
Generally, members appointed to the Pacific Symphony Board of Directors are expected to 
make substantial financial contributions and solicit contributions from other people as part of each 
member's ongoing duties. Instead of requiring the Mayor Pro Tern to make, or solicit the making 
of, contributions, Pacific Symphony plans to provide the Mayor Pro Tern with up to four tickets per 
performance for the purpose of him inviting other elected and governmental officials from Orange 
County for the purpose of exposing them to the educational and civic benefits that the Pacific 
Symphony provides to the region and its communities. Thus, not all Board members receive the 
same number of tickets. Other Board members make substantial contributions to Pacific Symphony 
and receive tickets when requested. 
The position held by the Mayor Pro Tern was made available by collaboration between the 
Pacific Symphony and the ACCOC. The Mayor Pro Tern applied for the position, along with other 
elected officials in Orange County, and was selected by the ACCOC to serve on the Pacific 
Symphony Board. The position would require significant time and effort by the Mayor Pro Tern to 
coordinate inviting and attending the events with other elected officials. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 82030 generally defines income as a payment received, including but not limited to 
any salary, wage, advance, gift, reimbursement for expenses or per diem. While income, other than 
a gift, is potentially reportable and may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of interest, the amount 
of income an official may receive from any particular source is not generally restricted under the 
Act. 


File No. A-18-205 
Page No. 3 
Alternatively, Section 82028 defines a gift as "any payment that confers a personal benefit 
on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received," and 
places the burden on the official to show that the official provided consideration of equal or greater 
value in return for the payment. Equal consideration means that each party receives benefits of 
equal value. (In re Burciaga (1975) 2 FPPC Ops. 17.) To the extent that the tickets are considered 
gifts, any tickets received and used by the Mayor Pro Tern, or transferred to his guests, are subject 
to the $470 gift limit (Section 89503; Regulation 18940.2), must be reported as gifts on his 
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700), if the tickets accumulate to $50 or more in value 
(Section 87207), and may result in a conflict of interest if the gifts from Pacific Symphony to the 
Mayor Pro Tern aggregate to $470 or more in value and he takes part in a governmental decision 
that would have a material financial effect on the nonprofit. (Sections 87100, 87103(e); Regulations 
18700, 18703.4). 
You have generally asked if inviting guests and educating guests on the Pacific Symphony's 
programs is consideration of equal or greater value for the receipt of the tickets. While the Mayor 
Pro Tern will be limited to four tickets per event, the number of events will not be limited, and the 
value of the events has not been specifically identified. 
As stated above, the Mayor Pro Tern has the burden of showing that he has provided equal 
or greater consideration under Section 82028. Considering the very nature of tickets for an 
entertainment event, the receipt of tickets by a public official must be weighed very carefully 
against the return consideration. In this case, merely coordinating invitations, attending 
performances, and educating his-invited guests on Pacific Symphony's programs and benefits do 
not appear to rise to the level of consideration of equal or greater value in exchange for four tickets 
to all Pacific Symphony performances. This conclusion is further supported by the fact that the 
tickets are provided with the intent that the Mayor Pro Tern invite other public officials. 
Accordingly, it appears that the tickets are offered to the Mayor Pro Tern specifically because of his 
official status and his connection to other elected officials, as opposed to in exchange for 
consideration. 
Finally, you have stated that other members of the Board may also receive tickets when 
requested, but are expected to make substantial financial contributions and solicit contributions 
from other people as part of each member's ongoing duties. The Mayor Pro Tern will be the only 
member of the Board provided tickets without making substantial financial contribution to the 
Board or soliciting contributions on behalf of the Board. Again, it appears that the Board is 
. providing a benefit to the Mayor Pro Tern primarily because of his official status, as opposed to a 
substantial engagement in community outreach, which is a benefit not provided to other members of 
the Board who appear to be providing even greater consideration. 
Absent additional facts, the tickets will be gifts to the Mayor Pro Tern and subject to the 
$470 gift limit.2 
2 Please note tickets must be reported as a gift only if used or transferred to another person. (Regulation 
18946. l(a).) Unused tickets do not constitute a gift to the Mayor Pro Tern. 


File No. A-18-205 
Page No. 4 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
SKP:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Counsel, Gegal Division
